findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Record reconcile_check.py as a second formal exemption in AC4 (alongside inbound_fields.py), and either add a follow-up ticket to route reconcile_check's vendor comparison through the Backend port's InboundMapper/OutboundMapper, or explicitly scope the 'zero vendor reach-through' success criterion to exclude the reconcile-check subcommand with a documented rationale.
evidence: reconcile_check.py:70: '_load_outbound_fields' returns _load_sibling('outbound_fields', 'adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py') — a function-nested lazy-load of a Jira adapter module
reconcile_check.py:241, 247: of._extract_jira_field(jira_issue, ...) and of._assignee_matches(...) — vendor-shaped comparison helpers called directly
test_backend_neutrality.py:94-99: 'reconcile_check.py is DELIBERATELY NOT covered here' — the gate uses module-level AST only and explicitly excludes this file
AC4 text: 'single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py' — reconcile_check.py is not listed
Success Criteria: 'no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific'
findings.finding: The epic's AC4 records only one exemption to the 'zero vendor reach-through' rule (inbound_fields.py), but reconcile_check.py contains a second, intentional vendor reach-through: it function-nested lazy-loads adapters/jira/outbound_fields.py and calls _extract_jira_field/_assignee_matches for field-level comparison. The neutrality gate test explicitly documents this as deliberate and out of scope (test_backend_neutrality.py:94-99). The AC as written ('every package-root *.py module has NO adapters.jira/acli_subprocess import ... single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py') and the success criterion ('no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific') are both falsified by reconcile_check.py in the reconcile-check mode execution path.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('Zero vendor reach-through in core'); Success Criteria ('no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific')
scenarios: A second backend implementor finds that reconcile-check mode is Jira-specific despite the epic claiming zero vendor reach-through, requiring a Jira-dependent environment to run the check subcommand

The AC passes CI (gate only checks module-level imports) while reconcile_check.py continues to lazy-load a Jira adapter at runtime — the documented exemption in the test is invisible to plan readers consulting only the epic's ACs
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, change 'single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py' to 'two recorded exemptions: inbound_fields.py (Jira InboundMapper implementation pending relocation, ticket 556a) and reconcile_check.py (uses vendor field-extraction helpers via lazy by-path load for comparison parity; routed through Backend port by follow-up ticket <new-id>).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either assign reconcile_check.py's _load_sibling removal to an explicit child (e.g., extend 97f2 or 625b scope) or add a second recorded exemption for it in parent AC4 with rationale.
evidence: 4af8: 'reconcile_check keeps its lazy internal-helper _load_sibling … fully relocating them … is a smaller separate follow-up. The neutrality gate documents this boundary explicitly.'
Parent AC4: 'every package-root … module has NO adapters.jira/acli_subprocess import … single recorded exemption: inbound_fields.py'
No sibling in the roster names reconcile_check.py as its scope.
findings.finding: reconcile_check.py's Jira-specific lazy `_load_sibling` import is explicitly documented as a residual in 4af8 ('not this ticket'), but NO sibling in the complete roster covers it, and parent AC4 records only ONE exemption (inbound_fields.py) with no carve-out for reconcile_check. This is a partial-without-remainder gap: 4af8 acknowledges the remainder but neither names the ticket that owns it nor claims it is exempt.
findings.location: child 4af8-2d88-719e-4478, Scope boundary section; parent AC4
scenarios: The neutrality gate added by 4af8 either (a) exempts reconcile_check silently, leaving a vendor reach-through that parent AC4 forbids, or (b) does not exempt it, causing the gate to remain red after all children land — blocking the epic's completion verifier.
findings.suggested_fix: Add reconcile_check.py to the scope of 97f2 (which already rewires scalar Jira imports in core) or create a micro-child for it; update the parent AC4 exemption list to either include it or confirm it will be resolved.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: etc.
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either prefix the 'LLM-Review +1 and Verified +1' sub-criterion with the `[operator-attested]` tag (so the rubric exemption applies) or replace it with a repo-verifiable proxy (e.g. 'merged to main' implies vote approval).
evidence: AC text: 'each landed with LLM-Review +1 and Verified +1'
No `[operator-attested]` tag on the checkbox

Rubric: 'classify a criterion whose checkbox text begins with the exact case-insensitive tag [operator-attested] as in-session completable when its done evidence inherently lives OUTSIDE the codebase'
findings.finding: AC bullet 6 includes 'each landed with LLM-Review +1 and Verified +1' as part of the completeness check, but this evidence lives entirely outside the repository (Gerrit vote records) and neither carries the `[operator-attested]` tag nor provides a deterministic repo-artifact or CI command through which an executor can verify it in-session.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — sixth bullet ('All src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py modules respect the 800-LOC module-size gate...')
scenarios: An executor running `make lint && make typecheck && make test` can verify CI green, but cannot confirm Gerrit votes from within the repo
A reviewer reading the closed PR has no repo artifact to inspect for the vote criterion — must manually check Gerrit
findings.suggested_fix: Change the AC line to: `[operator-attested] each landed child carries LLM-Review +1 and Verified +1 (Gerrit vote record on the linked change)` — or simply remove the sub-criterion and rely on 'merged to main' as the implicit proxy, since merges already require both votes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge or resolve the Jira-specific `jql` parameter name on `TicketTransport.search_issues` — either rename it to a neutral term (e.g. `query: str`), document it as a recorded known-residual exemption analogous to the `inbound_fields.py` exemption, or add it to an open child's scope so the port fully satisfies the plan's own success criterion of no Jira-specific names in core.
evidence: def search_issues(self, jql: str, start_at: int = 0, max_results: int = 50) -> list[dict[str, Any]]: ...

Plan Success Criteria: 'A second backend can be introduced by implementing `adapters/<x>/` against the Backend port alone: no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific.'
The port module is itself a core artifact — it lives at the package root as `_backend.py` and is explicitly described as the vendor-neutral interface.
No open child claims to neutralize this parameter name; no exemption is recorded analogous to the inbound_fields.py exemption.
findings.finding: TicketTransport.search_issues takes a parameter named `jql` — Jira Query Language is Jira-specific. The plan asserts the port is vendor-neutral and that a second backend needs only implement `adapters/<x>/` against it, but a GitHub/Linear backend would receive a `jql` parameter it cannot use. The plan has no child, exemption, or recorded residual for this specific Jira-named parameter on the primary transport method.
findings.location: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_backend.py:75 (TicketTransport.search_issues); Success Criteria section
scenarios: When implementing a GitHub Issues adapter, the implementor must accept a `jql` parameter on `search_issues` that has no GitHub equivalent, creating a false protocol contract.
A reviewer implementing a second backend reading the port protocol would conclude JQL syntax must be supported, leading to a translation shim that fabricates or ignores the parameter.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the parameter to `query: str` in the Protocol stub (a pure rename with no behavior change to the Jira implementation, since Python Protocol method params are structurally typed and the Jira AcliClient can keep its own `jql` name internally). Document the rename in the ADR §(d) interface table.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either rename `binding_store.get_jira_key()` to a neutral name (e.g. `get_remote_key()`), or record it as an explicit out-of-scope residual with a follow-up ticket (analogous to the `inbound_fields.py` / `556a` exemption), so the plan's Success Criteria and the open children's scopes are consistent.
evidence: def get_jira_key(self, local_id: str) -> str | None: at binding_store.py:258
Called at outbound_differ.py:531, outbound_differ.py:600, outbound_links.py:112, inbound_differ.py:666, run_differs.py:459 — all package-root core modules.
Plan Success Criteria: 'no package-root core module names, imports, lazy-loads, or shape-compares anything Jira-specific.'
Absence: no open child (97f2, canonical-shape outbound field diff, canonical link diff, absence probe, rich-text codec) lists binding_store.py or get_jira_key in its scope.
findings.finding: binding_store.get_jira_key() is a Jira-named method on a package-root core module, called from multiple other core modules (outbound_differ.py:531, outbound_links.py:112, inbound_differ.py:666, run_differs.py:459, binding_store.py:258). The plan's Success Criteria explicitly bars 'package-root core module names… anything Jira-specific,' but no open child claims to rename or route this method through the Backend port, and no exemption is recorded for it.
findings.location: src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/binding_store.py:258 (get_jira_key); Success Criteria section
scenarios: A second backend (GitHub) would bind a remote issue by its GitHub issue number, but the binding store only provides get_jira_key() — either the method name is wrong or a parallel get_github_key() method must be added, exactly the Jira-proliferation pattern the seam is meant to prevent.

The canonical-link-diff open child adds map_remote_links to SupportsLinks, but outbound_links.py still calls binding_store.get_jira_key() to resolve local deps to remote keys — the link diff cannot be made vendor-neutral while this call site remains.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ticket IDs to the four unnamed open children and add a note in each bullet (or in each child's file-impact list) identifying which child owns the corresponding `test_backend_neutrality.py` test cases.
evidence: Four open bullets in 'Child decomposition / Open' section have no ticket-ID prefix (unlike landed children which all have IDs like `ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb`)
Success Criteria: 'every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child'
AC bullet 4: 'verified by the union of AST/sweep cases in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py added by the open children'
findings.finding: The four open child bullets ('Canonical-shape outbound field diff', 'Canonical link diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port') carry no ticket IDs. The Success Criteria states 'every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child', but without IDs an executor cannot verify that invariant or confirm unambiguous ownership, and cannot determine which child is responsible for authoring each portion of `test_backend_neutrality.py`.
findings.location: Child decomposition — Open children (four unnamed/untracked bullets)
scenarios: Executor cannot confirm 'exactly one child per deliverable' without ticket IDs
Ownership of test_backend_neutrality.py cases is ambiguous — two children could duplicate or omit coverage
During review, a child PR cannot be cross-referenced to its parent epic deliverable
findings.suggested_fix: Prefix each open child bullet with its ticket ID (e.g. `xxxx-xxxx-xxxx-xxxx`) as done for landed children, and annotate each with 'owns neutrality test cases: <AST rule names>' to make the `test_backend_neutrality.py` coverage map unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Restate AC4 and AC5 in future-tense exit-criteria form (e.g., 'shall be verified by … on landing of the open children') to eliminate the contradiction with the decomposition's open-children list.
evidence: AC4: '…verified by the union of AST/sweep cases in tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/test_backend_neutrality.py added by the open children, green on main'
AC5: '…verified by the canonical-diff children's characterization and neutrality tests, green on main'
Decomposition: 'Open (the remaining neutrality work…)' lists canonical-diff, link-diff, absence-probe, and rich-text children as not yet landed
findings.finding: AC bullets 4 and 5 assert tests are 'green on main' but those tests are deliverables of open (future) children, contradicting the decomposition which lists these children as not yet landed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullets 4 ('verified by … neutrality tests, green on main') and 5 ('verified by the canonical-diff children's characterization and neutrality tests, green on main'); Child Decomposition — 'Open' section
scenarios: An implementer reading the AC believes the neutrality suite already passes on main and is startled to find the test file doesn't exist yet; a reviewer marks AC4/5 as pre-satisfied when they are actually exit criteria for future children.
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'green on main' in AC4 and AC5 to 'green on main upon landing of the respective open child' so the bullets read as landing gates rather than current assertions.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC bullets (or extend AC4/AC5) to explicitly cover the absence-probe migration (SupportsAbsenceProbe, probe.py, neutral ProbeBranch/ProbeResult) and the rich-text/comment-limits migration (FieldSanitizer routing in outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py), naming both removal of old coupling and replacement via port calls.
evidence: Body: 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe — Jira REST probe mechanics move to adapters/jira/probe.py; neutral ProbeBranch/ProbeResult vocabulary stays in core'
Body: 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port — outbound_comments.py and inbound_translate.py route through FieldSanitizer/InboundMapper'
No AC bullet references SupportsAbsenceProbe, ProbeBranch, ProbeResult, FieldSanitizer, probe.py, outbound_comments.py, or inbound_translate.py
findings.finding: The open children 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe' and 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port' are described as deliverables in the body but no AC bullet explicitly names or measures them; they are only indirectly swept by AC4's vendor-import ban.
findings.location: Child Decomposition — 'Open' section (absence probe, rich-text/comment children); Acceptance Criteria — all six bullets
scenarios: A reviewer cannot confirm that the absence-probe migration is complete because no AC states what 'done' looks like for that child beyond the generic sweep; the rich-text codec relocation has no stated verification other than lint/test green.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC4 (or add a dedicated bullet) to require that each migrated call site is verified as actually routing through the port — e.g., by a contract or integration test exercising the replacement path for each open child's migration, not only the absence of the old import.
evidence: AC4 text: 'every package-root … module has NO adapters.jira/acli_subprocess import … and NO … lazy-key literal' — purely an absence check
No AC bullet says 'port call present and exercised' for absence probe, rich-text, or link-diff migrations
AC5 covers canonical comparison for field/link differs but does not address absence-probe or rich-text codec replacement
findings.finding: AC4 verifies removal of vendor imports via the AST sweep but no AC explicitly verifies the replacement side (that the port call is actually wired in) for the open children's migrations, violating the migration criterion that both removal and replacement must be verified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — bullet 4 ('Zero vendor reach-through in core')
scenarios: A module could delete a Jira import and replace it with a stub or dead code path; the sweep test would pass while the actual port-based behavior is never invoked, and no AC would catch the gap.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan (or expand-contract sequence) for the open children's destructive steps: literal deletion sweep, sweep-gate enforcement, and removal of vendor comparison helpers from core — e.g., a note on which commits to revert atomically or a feature-flag fallback period.
evidence: 'a sweep gate bans the literal in package-root core' — no back-out procedure if the gate causes unexpected breakage
'vendor comparison helpers become adapter-internal' — once deleted from core and tests go green, recovery requires reverting multiple children
No rollback path is mentioned anywhere in the plan for the open children's destructive steps
Rubric: 'The remedy for a destructive/breaking change is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequencing'
findings.finding: The open children perform destructive, hard-to-reverse steps (deleting lazy-key literals from core, enforcing a sweep-gate ban, removing vendor comparison helpers from core) with no explicit rollback or back-out plan described. The rubric requires a reversibility mechanism beyond 'tests must be green'.
findings.location: Open children section / Acceptance Criteria — 'Zero vendor reach-through in core' and 'Canonical comparison' bullets
scenarios: A downstream integration test (not covered by unit suite) fails after the literal-deletion sweep; no documented back-out path slows recovery
A future child discovers that the canonical-diff approach cannot cover an edge case, requiring the vendor helper to be re-introduced; no expand-contract sequence is defined

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation deliverable for the `config.reconciler.backend` key (accepted values, default, and effect) to the plan — either an update to the operator/config reference doc, the README, or a config-schema annotation — so the new config surface is discoverable before the second backend epic lands.
evidence: 'Backend chosen via `config.reconciler.backend` through the registry; default `"jira"` reproduces prior behavior end-to-end (S3) — landed, change 902'
No reference to a README, operator guide, or config-schema doc update anywhere in the plan
The key is explicitly new (prior behavior had no explicit backend selector)
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` configuration key (landed in S3, change 902) is a user-facing config surface with no mention of updating user-facing configuration documentation (README, operator guide, or equivalent). Users who write config files need to know the key exists, its default value, and accepted values.
findings.location: Child S3 description: '`reconciler.backend` config + in-tree registry + `select_backend()`' / AC bullet for S3
scenarios: An operator upgrading to this version does not know the new key exists and cannot configure a non-default backend when it becomes available
Config schema tooling (JSON Schema, pydantic model docs) is not updated, causing validation errors or missing autocomplete for IDE users

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure that each open child introducing a new capability Protocol (SupportsAbsenceProbe, updated SupportsLinks, FieldSanitizer) either amends ADR 0035 §(d) or adds a sub-section documenting the rationale for splitting that capability as optional vs. mandatory on the base Backend port, so second-backend implementors have a clear specification.
evidence: Plan names SupportsAbsenceProbe, SupportsLinks gains map_remote_links + link_payload_for_relation, FieldSanitizer as new Protocol roles introduced by open children
ADR §(d) amendment is scoped to 'the canonical-comparison principle' only — no mention of documenting the rationale for capability Protocol decomposition (why optional vs. mandatory)
Absence of any statement that open-child Protocol additions will each be accompanied by an ADR/design-doc update
findings.finding: The open children each introduce new capability Protocols (SupportsAbsenceProbe, updated SupportsLinks with map_remote_links/link_payload_for_relation, FieldSanitizer) as new architectural extension points, but the plan does not specify that each new Protocol's rationale — why it is a separate optional capability vs. a mandatory port method — will be captured in ADR 0035 or any design doc. A future backend implementer needs this to correctly implement adapters/<x>/.
findings.location: Child decomposition — open children: 'Canonical link diff', 'Vendor absence probe behind SupportsAbsenceProbe', 'Rich-text codec + comment limits behind the port'
scenarios: A developer implementing a second backend (epic be74-7832-03a8-48ac) encounters SupportsAbsenceProbe and cannot determine from ADR 0035 alone whether their backend must implement it, what the fallback behavior is if it doesn't, or why it was split from the base port — leading to incorrect adapter implementations.
A reviewer of a future adapter PR has no documented rationale for the capability Protocol split and cannot verify the adapter is correctly scoped.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR 0035 §(d) amendment planned for the canonical-diff child, add a 'Capability Protocol decomposition principle' subsection explaining the rule for when a behavior is a separate optional Protocol (backend may not support it) vs. a required port method. Each open child that adds a Protocol should reference this rule in its ADR amendment rather than requiring a separate doc per child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the canonical-shape outbound diff child how fields emitted by OutboundMapper but NOT produced by InboundMapper (parent, reporter, and any others) are handled in the local-vs-local comparison — either extend InboundMapper to cover them, or document that those fields remain compared against Jira-shaped values via a narrowly-scoped residual path with an explicit rationale.
evidence: inbound_fields.py:177-180: map_remote_to_local returns dict with keys title/description/ticket_type/priority/status/assignee — no parent, reporter, or labels
outbound_fields.py:531-558: _diff_fields handles 'parent' by comparing local_val against jira_fields.get('parent')['key'] — a Jira-shaped nested dict not produced by InboundMapper
outbound_fields.py:346-366: _diff_reporter compares against jira_fields.get('reporter') accountId — also not produced by InboundMapper
Plan: 'the differ canonicalizes the snapshot via InboundMapper and diffs local-vs-local' — no mention of extended InboundMapper coverage or alternate handling for out-of-scope fields
findings.finding: The canonical-shape outbound field diff open child's mechanism has an unaddressed field-coverage asymmetry: InboundMapper.map_remote_to_local (inbound_fields.py) canonicalizes only the inbound-mirrored fields (title, description, ticket_type, priority, status, assignee), while OutboundMapper emits additional fields including 'parent' (compared as jira_fields["parent"]["key"] vs local resolved key), 'reporter' (accountId dict vs local string), and label-adjacent fields. After canonicalization, a local-vs-local diff would either omit these fields (silent regression: parent/reporter drift never detected) or compare local against a canonical-shape dict that simply has no key for them. The plan states 'vendor payload built only via the injected OutboundMapper at emission' but does not specify how the Jira-snapshot side of parent/reporter/labels is represented in the new local-vs-local comparison, nor whether InboundMapper will be extended to cover the full OutboundMapper field set.
findings.location: Open children section — 'Canonical-shape outbound field diff' bullet; also relevant to AC: 'Canonical comparison: the outbound field and link differs compare only canonical shapes'
scenarios: Parent sync regression: after canonicalization, parent key is absent from the canonicalized Jira shape, so parent divergence between local and Jira is never detected — every parent change is silently dropped
Reporter regression: reporter diff path disappears because InboundMapper produces no 'reporter' key in canonical shape
If InboundMapper is extended to cover these fields, the extension itself is a new unspecified design surface that may introduce its own vendor-shape exposure

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Mark 625b as depending on 4af8 (or at minimum document that 625b must be implemented after 4af8 is merged).
evidence: 625b: 'run_differs.py additionally passes inbound_mapper=backend.inbound to compute_outbound_mutations (the backend object is already in scope there — run_differs.py:481-495 currently passes outbound_mapper=backend.outbound)'
4af8 is listed as open; 625b does not declare a depends_on or ordering constraint on 4af8.
findings.finding: 625b assumes run_differs.py already has `outbound_mapper=backend.outbound` injected at line 481-495 (a state produced by 4af8), but 4af8 is open and no dependency ordering between them is stated. If 625b is implemented or reviewed against the pre-4af8 state of run_differs.py, the 'pass inbound_mapper=backend.inbound' step has no anchor point.
findings.location: child 625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f, Approach step 1 and Scope
scenarios: 625b implemented before 4af8: run_differs.py:481-495 does not yet have backend.outbound injection; 625b's step 1 has no valid anchor and the implementation breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add 'depends_on: 4af8-2d88-719e-4478' to 625b's context, or add an explicit ordering note: '625b requires 4af8 landed state in run_differs.py'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify aff0's ordering relative to 4af8 and update its run_differs.py wiring description to reflect the post-4af8 state (backend load already hoisted by 4af8's injection seam).
evidence: aff0: 'today the backend is loaded at run_differs.py:234 — AFTER the probe dispatch call at :230 — so the _load_reconcile_backend() call … is hoisted above the probe dispatch'
4af8 edits run_differs.py to inject outbound_mapper and inbound_mapper, changing the backend load site location and surrounding structure.
findings.finding: aff0's hoisting plan references run_differs.py:230 (probe dispatch) and :234 (backend load) as the pre-hoist line numbers, but 4af8 (open) substantially rewires run_differs.py (injecting mapper entrypoints, establishing backend.outbound/backend.inbound bindings). After 4af8 lands, aff0's stated line numbers and 'the backend object is already in scope there' assumption may no longer match the actual run_differs.py structure, making aff0's implementation plan stale.
findings.location: child aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a, Approach section (Wiring paragraph)
scenarios: After 4af8 lands, the backend may already be loaded before line :230 (because 4af8 needs it earlier); aff0's hoist is then either a no-op or conflicts with 4af8's insertion, producing a double-load or incorrect dispatch order.
findings.suggested_fix: State explicitly that aff0 must be implemented after 4af8 and that the backend instance from 4af8's injection seam is reused for the probe dispatch (no second load needed).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ordering note to 21ca that it should be implemented after 4af8 (or at minimum acknowledge that 21ca introduces the select_backend pattern in outbound_comments.py independently if it lands first).
evidence: 21ca: 'outbound_comments.py resolves the configured backend's sanitizer via the registry seam (select_backend(load_config()) — pattern proven at outbound_differ.py:450-454)'
4af8 is the child that establishes that pattern in outbound_differ.py; 4af8 is open and 21ca does not state a dependency on it.
findings.finding: 21ca's outbound_comments.py rewiring depends on the `select_backend(load_config())` pattern being 'proven at outbound_differ.py:450-454', a state that exists only after 4af8 lands. 21ca does not declare an ordering constraint on 4af8, creating an implicit dependency gap.
findings.location: child 21ca-cdb4-671c-460c, Approach section
scenarios: 21ca implemented before 4af8: the reference pattern at outbound_differ.py:450-454 does not yet exist; 21ca's implementation has no established precedent to follow and may introduce the pattern inconsistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the required landing order for run_differs.py edits (4af8 first, then aff0, then 625b) and retract the 'parallel-safe' claim for these three children, or consolidate the run_differs.py changes into a single child.
evidence: 4af8 Scope: 'run_differs: production injection point — obtains the configured Backend once and threads backend.outbound / backend.inbound'
aff0 Scope: 'run_differs.py (capability-guarded routing at the probe call site)' + hoisting _load_reconcile_backend()
625b Approach step 1: 'run_differs.py additionally passes inbound_mapper=backend.inbound to compute_outbound_mutations'
Parent says open children are 'disjoint edit-sets … parallel-safe' — this is not true for run_differs.py.
findings.finding: Three open children — 4af8, aff0, and 625b — all edit run_differs.py with no stated ordering or merge strategy among them. 4af8 injects mapper entrypoints and establishes backend bindings; aff0 hoists the backend load and replaces the probe dispatch; 625b adds inbound_mapper passing. These are non-trivially overlapping edits to the same function bodies in run_differs.py.
findings.location: child 4af8-2d88-719e-4478 (run_differs.py mapper injection); child aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a (backend hoist + probe routing); child 625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f (inbound_mapper pass-through)
scenarios: All three children developed in parallel produce conflicting edits to run_differs.py that cannot be merged without full re-review; or the children land in an order that leaves run_differs.py in an inconsistent intermediate state.

evidence: Session log ticket graph lists five open children each with a formal ticket ID: 625b, eefd, aff0, 21ca, 97f2.
Plan's open-child section names only 97f2 by ticket ID; the other four children are described by prose alone with no ticket ID anchor.

Success Criteria states 'Every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child' but without IDs for four of the five open children there is no way to verify the 1:1 correspondence or audit additional constraints those tickets may carry.
findings.finding: Four of the five open children from the session log's formal ticket graph (625b-9cd1-1ce9-4e5f, eefd-49b1-995f-488d, aff0-b0d9-e11c-4b6a, 21ca-cdb4-671c-460c) are described in the plan by content but their ticket identifiers are silently omitted, making the plan's Success Criteria claim ('Every deliverable above is covered by exactly one child') unauditable against the ticket graph.
scenarios: A reviewer or CI gate checking the ticket graph cannot confirm that the plan's unnamed 'Canonical link diff' child maps to eefd rather than some other ticket, or that eefd has no additional constraints beyond the plan's prose.
If any of the four unnamed tickets carries acceptance criteria that differ from or extend the plan's prose description, those constraints are invisible to implementers reading only the plan.
During stacked Gerrit landing, the absence of ticket IDs for four open children makes it impossible to auto-link landed changes back to the correct child tickets for sign-off.

findings.finding: S3's file_impact list includes 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' (reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira') and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' (reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key') — but the S3 description body explicitly states: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error.' The contradictory file_impact entries will cause an implementer following the file list to open and edit the wrong files.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) file_impact list vs. description body
findings.suggested_fix: Replace S3 file_impact entries: remove `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`; add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with reason 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'.

findings.finding: S3's (94f9) file_impact metadata lists 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' as edit targets, directly contradicting the story description body which states 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the real config home is the app-level src/rebar/_config_schema.py.' An implementer following the file_impact would edit the wrong files.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 (file_impact metadata vs. description body)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the two wrong file_impact entries with: {path: 'src/rebar/_config_schema.py', reason: 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'}.

findings.finding: S2 explicitly assigns rewiring of three IdentityConvention call sites (dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, binding_walk.py:341) to S4, but S4's confirmed leak-site list and scope do not include those three files — creating an owned-by-none gap that leaves inlined Jira-specific label logic in backend-neutral core and undermines PAC-7's 'no core edit required' claim for a second backend.
findings.location: child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 (Approach, IdentityConvention section); child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 (Scope, confirmed leak sites list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add dispatch_one.py:320, binding_store.py:516, and binding_walk.py:341 to S4's 'Confirmed leak sites' as IdentityConvention adoption sites (distinct from vendor-import sites), include them in S4's neutrality gate, and extend the contract suite to verify that no core module inlines the rebar-id label format.
